stem: To apply the Limit Comparison Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n}{[[c]]n^{3}+1} \) against the benchmark series \( \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{1}{n} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{([[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n)/([[c]]n^{3}+1)}{1/n} \).
solution: \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{n([[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n)}{[[c]]n^{3}+1} = \lim_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{[[a]]n^{3}+[[b]]n^{2}}{[[c]]n^{3}+1} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[c]]} = [[answer]] \), a finite positive number, so \( \sum a_n \) and \( \sum 1/n \) share the same convergence behavior.
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the leading coefficients.
This wrongly assumes the numerator's lower-degree terms make the ratio vanish.
This multiplies the leading coefficients together instead of taking their ratio.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: To apply the Limit Comparison Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{[[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n+1}} \) against the benchmark series \( \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{1}{n} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]/\sqrt{[[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n+1}}{1/n} \).
solution: \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{[[c]]n}{\sqrt{[[a]]n^{2}+[[b]]n+1}} = \dfrac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{[[a]]}} = [[answer]] \), a finite positive number, so \( \sum a_n \) and \( \sum 1/n \) share the same convergence behavior.
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( [[a]] \) directly instead of by \( \sqrt{[[a]]} \), forgetting the square root survives the limit.
This inverts the correct ratio, multiplying by \( \sqrt{[[a]]} \) instead of dividing by it.
This wrongly assumes the radical in the denominator dominates and sends the ratio to \( 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: To apply the Limit Comparison Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n+[[b]]}{[[k]]^{n}} \) against the benchmark series \( \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{n}{[[k]]^{n}} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{([[a]]n+[[b]])/[[k]]^{n}}{n/[[k]]^{n}} \).
solution: The \( [[k]]^{n} \) factors cancel exactly, leaving \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n+[[b]]}{n} = [[a]] = [[answer]] \), a finite positive number, so \( \sum a_n \) and \( \sum n/[[k]]^{n} \) share the same convergence behavior.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the exponential factors do not cancel and instead force the ratio to \( 0 \).
This wrongly reintroduces a factor of \( [[k]] \) after the exponential terms have already cancelled.
This divides by \( [[k]] \) as though the exponential factors only partially cancelled.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.

stem: To apply the Limit Comparison Test to \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n + [[c]]\ln n}{n^{2}} \) against the benchmark series \( \displaystyle\sum \dfrac{1}{n} \), compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{([[a]]n+[[c]]\ln n)/n^{2}}{1/n} \).
solution: \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{[[a]]n+[[c]]\ln n}{n} = [[a]] + [[c]]\lim_{n\to\infty}\dfrac{\ln n}{n} = [[a]] + 0 = [[answer]] \), a finite positive number, since \( \ln n \) grows slower than \( n \), so \( \sum a_n \) and \( \sum 1/n \) share the same convergence behavior.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the logarithmic term dominates and drives the whole ratio to \( 0 \).
This wrongly assumes the logarithmic term grows faster than \( n \), sending the limit to infinity.
This adds the coefficients \( [[a]] \) and \( [[c]] \) directly, as if \( \ln n/n \) tended to \( 1 \) instead of \( 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.
